heroOverview: Intussusception occurs when one segment of the intestine telescopes into an adjacent downstream segment, creating an obstruction that cuts off both the passage of bowel contents and the blood supply to the trapped loop. In children under two years it is the most common cause of acute intestinal obstruction and must be treated within hours to prevent bowel infarction. The classic triad of sudden colicky abdominal pain, a palpable sausage-shaped abdominal mass, and passage of bloody mucus ('redcurrant jelly' stool) is seen in fewer than 50% of children; inconsolable crying with drawn-up knees and pallor is the practical early presentation. Adults account for only 5% of all cases but, in contrast to children, more than 90% of adult intussusceptions have a demonstrable lead point — most often a polyp, a tumour, or a Meckel's diverticulum — making a structural cause assessment mandatory. Non-operative air or hydrostatic enema reduction is successful in 70-90% of uncomplicated paediatric cases, and surgery is reserved for failure, perforation, or confirmed bowel necrosis.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence of approximately 56 per 100,000 infants annually in the United States; peak incidence at 5-9 months of age
keyStats.demographics: 80% of cases occur in children under two; male-to-female ratio approximately 4:3 in children; adult cases represent about 5% of all intussusceptions and 1-3% of adult bowel obstructions
keyStats.avgAge: Peak in infants 5-9 months; can occur at any age; a second smaller peak in adults aged 50-80 when structural lead points are most common
keyStats.globalCases: Intussusception is reported worldwide with similar incidence across regions; rates as high as 65 per 100,000 children under one year have been recorded in some populations

definition: Intussusception (ICD-10: K56.1) is an acquired mechanical bowel obstruction in which a proximal segment of intestine (the intussusceptum) invaginates into the lumen of the immediately distal segment (the intussuscipiens), dragging its mesentery with it. This produces a triple-walled cylinder of bowel. The compressed mesentery impairs venous outflow first, causing oedema and engorgement; if the process continues, arterial inflow is compromised, progressing to mucosal sloughing, haemorrhage, and ultimately full-thickness bowel necrosis and perforation. The ileocolic region — where the ileum enters the caecum through the ileocaecal valve — is the most common site, accounting for approximately 75-95% of paediatric cases. In children, the majority are idiopathic; hypertrophy of Peyer's patches following a viral upper respiratory infection or gastroenteritis is the accepted mechanism — the enlarged lymphoid tissue acts as a lead point. In adults, a structural lesion drives the intussusception in more than 90% of cases; malignant tumours account for approximately half of adult structural lead points, with primary small-bowel tumours, colorectal cancer, and metastatic disease most commonly implicated. Classification by site distinguishes ileo-ileal, ileo-colic, colo-colic, and rare ileo-ileo-colic types. The condition is a surgical emergency when reduction is incomplete, when perforation is present, or when bowel viability is compromised.

typesClassification.description: The ileum telescopes into the colon across the ileocaecal valve; accounts for 75-95% of paediatric cases and is the form most amenable to enema reduction. The ileocaecal valve resists further passage and forms the apex of the intussuscepted segment.

Confined entirely to the small intestine, it is more common in adults and almost always has a structural lead point (Meckel's diverticulum, polyp, or lymphoma). It is not amenable to enema reduction and requires surgery.

One segment of colon telescopes into an adjacent colon segment; most often associated with a colonic polyp or carcinoma in adults. It can sometimes be reduced by enema in selected cases but usually requires surgical resection in adults.

No structural lead point is identified in approximately 90% of children under five. Hypertrophy of Peyer's patches triggered by viral gastroenteritis or an upper respiratory infection is the prevailing explanation.

A recognised complication of abdominal surgery in children, usually occurring within the first two weeks; typically involves a short ileo-ileal segment and presents with signs of small-bowel obstruction rather than the classic colicky pain picture.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset severe colicky abdominal pain that comes in waves every 15-20 minutes and then eases between episodes, causing an infant to draw up the knees and cry inconsolably, then appear briefly normal when the spasm passes — the most characteristic early symptom.
Vomiting that begins as a reflex response to pain and progressively becomes bilious as the bowel obstruction matures, indicating that the blockage is complete.

Passage of blood-stained mucus per rectum ('redcurrant jelly' stool), appearing hours after onset as the compressed mucosa sloughs and bleeds; its absence in early cases should not reassure clinicians.
Pallor and limpness between pain episodes in infants, sometimes mistaken for lethargy or altered consciousness; these children can appear critically unwell.
A palpable firm, sausage-shaped mass felt in the right upper or mid abdomen, representing the coiled loop of intussuscepted bowel — present in approximately 60% of cases on careful examination.
Abdominal distension that increases as the obstruction progresses and gas and fluid accumulate proximal to the blocked segment.
Absence of faeces or flatus beyond the initial passage of material distal to the obstruction, indicating complete bowel blockage.
High fever in cases that have progressed to bowel ischaemia or perforation, reflecting peritoneal inflammation or systemic sepsis.
Dehydration manifest as dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, and decreased urine output, from repeated vomiting combined with fluid shifts into the obstructed gut.

In adults: less dramatic presentation often including chronic or intermittent right-sided abdominal pain, partial obstruction symptoms, and anaemia or weight loss from an underlying tumour acting as the lead point.
earlyWarningSigns: Episodic, intense crying that subsides completely for 15-20 minutes then recurs without an obvious trigger in a previously well infant aged 4-12 months.
Drawing up the legs and arching the back with each episode of distress.
Passing normal or slightly mucoid stool early in the episode, followed by none further.
A brief period of unusual paleness and quiet — almost as if the infant has relaxed — between colicky spells.
emergencySigns: Passage of dark red bloody mucus per rectum combined with abdominal distension and persistent pain, indicating established vascular compromise.
Shock-like state — grey or mottled skin colour, rapid weak pulse, cold extremities, and reduced consciousness — from intestinal ischaemia or sepsis.
Abdominal rigidity or guarding, suggesting peritoneal irritation from perforation or full-thickness necrosis.
Prolonged altered consciousness or seizure-like episodes; intussusception is a known and underrecognised cause of acute encephalopathy in infants.

causes.cause: Hypertrophy of Peyer's patches after viral infection (paediatric)
causes.description: Viral upper respiratory infections and gastroenteritis — most often caused by adenovirus, rotavirus, or human herpesvirus 6 — trigger enlargement of the lymphoid tissue (Peyer's patches) in the terminal ileum wall. These hypertrophied patches act as lead points that peel the bowel wall inward with peristalsis. This is the mechanism in approximately 90% of children under five.

causes.cause: Structural lead point (adult and older children)
causes.description: Any focal mass attached to or within the bowel wall can be caught by the peristaltic wave and dragged downstream, inverting the surrounding bowel. Common lead points include Meckel's diverticulum, lipoma, hamartoma in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, lymphoma of the small bowel, metastatic melanoma, and primary colorectal carcinoma.

A Meckel's diverticulum — a remnant of the vitello-intestinal duct present in approximately 2% of the population — can become inflamed, inverted, or serve as a lead point that initiates ileo-ileal or ileocolic telescoping. It is the most common structural lead point in children aged 2-12.

The original 1998 rotavirus vaccine (Rotashield) was withdrawn because of a significantly increased risk of intussusception approximately one to two weeks after vaccination. Licensed current rotavirus vaccines (Rotarix, RotaTeq) carry a very small absolute risk of approximately 1-2 excess cases per 100,000 doses, which is far outweighed by rotavirus disease prevented.

causes.cause: Post-operative adhesions and bowel wall changes
causes.description: Surgical sites alter bowel wall compliance and mobility; post-operative intussusception, typically ileo-ileal, is a recognised complication within the first two weeks after abdominal surgery in children, driven by altered peristaltic coordination rather than a fixed lead point.

Intestinal lymphoma, particularly Burkitt lymphoma in children and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in adults, produces transmural tumour masses that readily act as lead points; intussusception may be the presenting event of previously unsuspected lymphoma.

riskFactors.description: The risk is highest in the first year of life and drops sharply after age two; the relative abundance of lymphoid tissue in the terminal ileum at this developmental stage is the biological basis.
Boys are affected approximately 1.3 times more often than girls in the paediatric age group, for reasons that are not fully understood.

riskFactors.factor: Recent viral gastroenteritis or upper respiratory tract infection
riskFactors.description: A preceding viral illness is present in about 25-40% of paediatric intussusception cases; the temporal link to Peyer's patch hypertrophy is well established.
Congenitally present in 2% of the population, it significantly increases intussusception risk in children aged 2-12 and is one of the most common lead points found at operation.
Any lesion projecting into the bowel lumen raises adult intussusception risk; patients with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome have a 50% lifetime risk of intussusception from hamartomatous polyps.
Post-operative intussusception within two weeks of laparotomy or laparoscopy, particularly in children, is a well-recognised clinical entity requiring a high index of suspicion.
Children with cystic fibrosis are at increased risk of ileocolic intussusception because inspissated intestinal secretions can create a faecal mass that serves as a lead point.

affectedDemographics: Infants aged 5-9 months — the single highest-risk group
Children under two years old for the great majority of paediatric cases
Boys slightly more than girls in childhood
Adults aged 50-80 with colorectal or small-bowel tumours
People with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome or familial polyposis throughout adulthood
Post-operative patients within two weeks of abdominal surgery
diagnosisOverview: Intussusception is a clinical and imaging diagnosis requiring urgent evaluation; any infant with episodic colicky pain, pallor, and vomiting should be assessed promptly. Abdominal ultrasound is the diagnostic investigation of choice in most paediatric emergency departments, with a sensitivity exceeding 95% and specificity above 88% in experienced hands; the pathognomonic 'target' or 'doughnut' sign — concentric rings of bowel representing the intussusceptum within the intussuscipiens — is reliably identified. Doppler ultrasonography can additionally assess mesenteric blood flow and help guide the decision between non-operative reduction and surgery: absent intramural flow indicates ischaemia. Plain abdominal radiographs are not sensitive enough to confirm the diagnosis but may show a soft-tissue mass in the right upper quadrant, absent caecal gas, small-bowel obstruction pattern, or free air if perforation has occurred. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen is the standard investigation in adults and in older children, providing both diagnostic confirmation and characterisation of any lead point. The diagnostic CT appearance — a multilayered 'target' lesion with fat stranding — reliably identifies the type and site of intussusception and informs surgical planning. Air or water-soluble contrast enema is simultaneously diagnostic and therapeutic in children without peritonitis, and is typically performed under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance in a radiology suite with surgical backup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line diagnostic test in children: identifies the characteristic target sign, locates the apex, and assesses blood flow to detect ischaemia before enema reduction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the abdomen and a probe is passed over it; the procedure takes 10-15 minutes, is painless, uses no radiation, and requires no preparation. The target sign is usually obvious to experienced sonographers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Simultaneously diagnostic and therapeutic: pressurised air or fluid is introduced rectally to push the intussusceptum back out of the intussuscipiens. It is the treatment of choice in stable children without peritonitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small soft tube is placed in the rectum; air or liquid is gently instilled while a radiologist or radiographer monitors on fluoroscopy or ultrasound. The child lies still, usually without sedation; a nurse and a surgeon are present. The procedure takes 20-40 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line in adults and in children where ultrasound is non-diagnostic; characterises the type, site, and length of intussusception and identifies any lead-point mass.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan with an intravenous contrast injection through a cannula; you lie still inside a ring-shaped scanner. Results are typically available within an hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quick initial assessment for free air (perforation), bowel obstruction pattern, or soft-tissue density in the right abdomen; not sensitive enough to diagnose or exclude intussusception.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two brief X-rays of the abdomen (supine and erect or decubitus), taking one to two minutes without sedation or contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive for failed enema reduction, peritoneal signs, perforation, or adult cases where resection of the lead point is required.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general anaesthesia; laparoscopy involves three small incisions and recovery within days; open laparotomy requires a larger incision with a recovery of five to seven days in hospital.

treatmentOverview: Management depends on the patient's age, stability, and whether perforation or ischaemia is present. For stable children without peritonitis or perforation, non-operative pneumatic (air) enema reduction is the standard treatment of choice at most paediatric centres; success rates range from 70 to 90% with experienced radiologists. Hydrostatic enema using water-soluble contrast or saline under ultrasound guidance achieves comparable results. A second enema attempt following initial failure increases total success rates to 85-95% in uncomplicated cases. After successful reduction, children are observed for four to six hours for recurrence before discharge; the recurrence rate is approximately 10-15% after non-operative reduction. Surgery is required when enema reduction fails, when peritonitis is present, when there are signs of bowel necrosis, or in virtually all adult cases where a pathological lead point must be resected. Surgical reduction involves manual compression ('milking' the intussuscepted loop from the distal bowel end back to normal position), and is combined with resection of any lead point. If the bowel is necrotic or irreducible, resection with primary anastomosis or temporary stoma formation is performed. Antibiotics covering bowel flora are given perioperatively. In adults, the decision to attempt pre-operative enema reduction is guided by CT findings; when malignancy is likely, upfront resection avoids tumour spillage and provides oncological margins. Post-operative care includes bowel rest, nasogastric decompression, and gradual reintroduction of feeds once bowel function returns.

medicalTreatments.name: Pneumatic (air) enema reduction
medicalTreatments.description: Room air is instilled rectally under controlled pressure (typically not exceeding 120 mmHg) while the abdomen is monitored fluoroscopically or with ultrasound, gradually pushing the intussusceptum back to its normal position. A pop of air into the small bowel and free flow of air through the ileocaecal valve confirms successful reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Success rates of 70-90% in uncomplicated paediatric ileocolic intussusception; second attempts raise combined success to 85-95%. Perforation rate is approximately 0.5-1%.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrostatic (saline or contrast) enema reduction
medicalTreatments.description: Warm saline or water-soluble contrast is used instead of air; allows real-time ultrasound guidance without radiation exposure, making it preferable in some centres. The technique is comparable in success and complication rates to air enema.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Success rates 70-85% in first attempts; similar overall efficacy to pneumatic reduction with a comparable perforation risk of under 1%.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation and bowel rest
medicalTreatments.description: All patients with established bowel obstruction require IV fluids to correct dehydration and electrolyte imbalances before reduction attempts; a nasogastric tube is placed to decompress the obstructed bowel and reduce vomiting aspiration risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential supportive measure; fluid resuscitation reduces the risk of haemodynamic deterioration during enema procedure and anaesthetic induction.

medicalTreatments.name: Perioperative broad-spectrum antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Antibiotics covering aerobic and anaerobic gram-negative bowel organisms are given before any surgical reduction or resection to reduce infection risk, particularly when bowel viability is uncertain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Perioperative antibiotics reduce post-operative wound infection by approximately 50% in bowel surgery per established surgical quality guidelines.

surgicalOptions.name: Manual surgical reduction (milking)
surgicalOptions.description: The surgeon gently compresses and pushes the intussusceptum backwards from the distal end, reversing the telescoping without bowel resection. Performed laparoscopically in stable cases or open when laparoscopy is contraindicated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful in approximately 70-80% of surgical cases; abandoned if the bowel tears or if necrosis is found, proceeding to resection.

surgicalOptions.name: Bowel resection with primary anastomosis
surgicalOptions.description: When the intussusceptum cannot be reduced manually, when bowel necrosis is present, or when a pathological lead point requires oncological resection, the affected segment is removed and the bowel ends are joined immediately. In adults with colonic involvement this usually means right hemicolectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive treatment with a 95-98% bowel continuity restoration rate in stable patients; anastomotic leak occurs in approximately 2-5% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Resection with temporary stoma formation
surgicalOptions.description: When peritoneal contamination from perforation, patient instability, or bowel condition makes primary anastomosis unsafe, the proximal bowel is brought to the skin as an ileostomy or colostomy. A second planned operation reconnects the bowel after recovery, typically three to six months later.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Used in high-risk cases to minimise anastomotic leak risk; stoma reversal is successful in approximately 80-90% of planned cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting for spontaneous reduction
alternativeTreatments.description: In very rare, mild, chronic partial intussusception in adults where symptoms are minimal and CT shows no vascular compromise, short-term observation is sometimes described in case reports. This is not standard practice and should not be applied to children or to any case with significant obstruction signs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Delay reduction for analgesia and fluid optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Not an alternative to reduction but an important adjunct: ensuring adequate pain relief with IV opioids and correction of dehydration before enema or surgical reduction improves procedure safety and success rates.

whySeeSpecialist: Intussusception requires immediate assessment in a paediatric emergency department or a centre with paediatric surgical expertise. Delay beyond six hours from symptom onset significantly raises the rate of bowel necrosis. Any adult diagnosed with intussusception on CT requires urgent surgical gastroenterology or colorectal surgery review because of the high probability of a malignant lead point. Non-operative enema reduction must be performed by a radiologist experienced in the technique with immediate surgical backup.
doctorSelectionGuide: For children, a paediatric emergency department staffed by paediatric surgeons and interventional radiologists experienced in enema reduction provides the best outcomes. For adults, a colorectal surgeon with access to laparoscopic resection and oncological expertise is essential, since malignancy is the leading cause. Centres that perform both diagnostic ultrasound and therapeutic enema in the same radiology session minimise delay and maximise success.
preventionStrategies: Ensure children receive the recommended rotavirus vaccine series, which dramatically reduces the viral gastroenteritis that predisposes to idiopathic intussusception — despite a very small and well-characterised vaccination-associated risk.
In adults with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome or familial polyposis, regular endoscopic polypectomy removes the hamartomatous polyps that act as lead points before they can cause intussusception.
Early diagnosis and treatment of intestinal lymphoma and colorectal cancer prevents these tumours from growing large enough to act as lead points.

Parents and caregivers of infants should be counselled about the early symptoms of intussusception — episodic crying with drawn-up knees and pallor — so that medical attention is sought before bowel ischaemia develops.
lifestyleModifications: For children recovering from intussusception, gradual reintroduction of oral feeds after successful reduction, starting with clear fluids and advancing to regular diet as tolerated.
After surgical resection, a low-residue diet for four to six weeks allows the bowel anastomosis to heal without excessive peristaltic stress.
Parents of children with a previous episode should be counselled that the 10-15% recurrence rate means any return of episodic crying and pallor warrants prompt re-evaluation.
Adults with an identified underlying lead point lesion should follow their treating specialist's dietary and activity advice until the primary pathology is definitively treated.

recommended: Clear fluids initially after successful enema reduction, advancing to regular diet within 12-24 hours if no recurrence
Post-surgical low-residue diet (white rice, white bread, cooked vegetables without skins, lean protein) for four to six weeks
Adequate hydration to maintain bowel motility and prevent inspissated stool, which can be a contributing factor in cystic fibrosis-related intussusception
Regular meals rather than large infrequent ones, to maintain predictable peristaltic patterns
avoid: High-fibre foods for four to six weeks after bowel surgery until anastomosis is confirmed healed
Large volumes in single meals immediately after reduction or surgery
Carbonated drinks in the first 24-48 hours post-reduction, as gas can distend the recently reduced segment

exerciseGuidelines: Children typically recover rapidly after successful enema reduction and can return to normal activity within 24-48 hours once tolerating feeds. After laparoscopic surgery, light activity is resumed within one to two weeks; after open laparotomy, return to full activity takes four to six weeks depending on the extent of resection. Heavy lifting and contact sports should be avoided for six weeks after any abdominal surgery.
dailyManagement: After non-operative reduction, observe the child for a minimum of four to six hours in hospital for signs of recurrence or failed reduction.
Return to the emergency department immediately if colicky pain, pallor, or bloody stool recur within 24-72 hours of discharge — recurrence happens in 10-15% of cases, almost always within 48 hours.

Adults awaiting follow-up surgery or oncology consultation after intussusception due to a tumour should have any worsening obstructive symptoms (increased pain, inability to pass stool, vomiting) reviewed urgently.
Ensure follow-up imaging or endoscopy is arranged to confirm the nature of any structural lead point found on the initial CT, so definitive management can be planned.
prognosis: Outcomes for paediatric intussusception are excellent when diagnosed and treated within 24 hours of symptom onset: mortality in high-income countries is below 0.1%, bowel necrosis requiring resection occurs in fewer than 10% of promptly treated cases, and most children are discharged within one to two days after successful enema reduction. Recurrence after non-operative reduction occurs in approximately 10-15% of cases; a further recurrence after a second reduction occurs in about 5%, and three or more recurrences may prompt surgical exploration to exclude an occult lead point. In adults, prognosis depends entirely on the underlying cause: intussusception caused by benign polyps or Meckel's diverticulum carries an excellent outcome after surgical resection, while those due to malignancy carry the prognosis of the primary tumour. Delay in diagnosis is the primary determinant of poor outcome: bowel necrosis requiring resection, post-operative complications, and longer hospitalisation all increase substantially with each additional hour of untreated ischaemia.
recoveryTimeline: After successful enema reduction in children, return to feeding within four to six hours and discharge within 24 hours are typical in uncomplicated cases. Post-laparoscopic surgery patients are usually discharged within two to four days and return to full activity within two weeks. After open resection with anastomosis, the hospital stay is five to seven days, and full recovery takes four to six weeks. Adults undergoing resection for a malignant lead point follow the oncological recovery pathway of the primary tumour.
complications: Bowel necrosis and perforation — the most feared complication, occurring when vascular compromise persists for more than six to twelve hours; requires resection and carries significant morbidity.
Recurrence after successful enema reduction in approximately 10-15% of paediatric cases, almost always within 72 hours; managed with repeat enema in most cases.
Enema-related perforation — a rare complication occurring in approximately 0.5-1% of reduction attempts, requiring emergency surgery.
Post-operative surgical site infection, anastomotic leak, or small-bowel obstruction from adhesions — standard surgical complications managed with antibiotics, drainage, or reoperation.
Occult malignancy in adult cases — a life-limiting complication when the lead point proves to be a primary bowel cancer or metastatic disease detected late.

supportResources.name: Boston Children's Hospital — Intussusception
supportResources.description: Parent-focused information on intussusception symptoms, diagnosis, and enema reduction from one of the world's leading paediatric surgical centres.

supportResources.name: NHS — Intussusception
supportResources.description: UK patient information covering signs, investigation, and management of intussusception in children and adults.

supportResources.name: Cincinnati Children's Hospital — Intussusception
supportResources.description: Detailed parent guide to intussusception with information about the enema procedure and what to expect after treatment.

sources.title: Mandeville K et al. Intussusception: Clinical Presentations and Imaging Characteristics. Pediatric Emergency Care, 2012
Waseem M, Rosenberg HK. Intussusception. Pediatric Emergency Care, 2008
Rao PM, Rhea JT. Intussusception in Adults. Radiology, 1999
WHO — Rotavirus vaccines: WHO position paper (2013)
Nguyen TN, Weiner TM. Adult Intussusception. Journal of the American College of Surgeons, 2011
Buettcher M et al. Intussusception in Children: Review of 42 Cases. Pediatric Surgery International, 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Volvulus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Volvulus is a twisting rather than telescoping of the bowel; both cause acute obstruction and bowel ischaemia but volvulus is more common in the sigmoid colon in adults and the midgut in neonates, while intussusception classically affects the ileocaecal region and is the more common cause in young infants.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute appendicitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Appendicitis causes constant right lower quadrant pain that migrates from the periumbilical area, without the characteristic episodic colicky pain-free intervals or bloody mucus of intussusception. Ultrasound readily distinguishes them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intestinal adhesions with complete obstruction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adhesion obstruction is the most common cause of adult small-bowel obstruction and typically follows prior surgery; CT distinguishes it from intussusception by showing the transition point without the characteristic target-sign cylinder.

confusedWithConditions.name: Paralytic ileus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Paralytic ileus causes uniform bowel dilation and absent peristalsis without a mechanical point of obstruction; it lacks the episodic colicky pain of intussusception and is not associated with the target sign on imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Volvulus
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are mechanical bowel obstructions requiring urgent intervention; they share the complication of intestinal ischaemia and necrosis if not treated quickly.

relatedConditions.name: Intestinal adhesions with complete obstruction
relatedConditions.relevance: Adhesion-related obstruction is the main differential diagnosis in adults presenting with acute small-bowel obstruction alongside intussusception.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Colorectal cancer is the most common malignant lead point for adult colonic intussusception; adult intussusception is considered a presentation of colorectal cancer until proven otherwise.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Crohn's transmural bowel wall thickening and inflammatory mass can act as a lead point for intussusception in older children and adults, and is one of the secondary causes to exclude at surgery.

faqs.question: What is intussusception?
faqs.answer: Intussusception is when one part of the intestine slides inside the next segment, like a telescope collapsing, blocking the bowel and cutting off its blood supply. It is the most common cause of bowel obstruction in children under two. In adults it is rare but almost always has an underlying cause such as a polyp or tumour.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of intussusception in a baby?
faqs.answer: A baby with intussusception typically cries inconsolably in sudden severe episodes lasting one to two minutes, then goes quiet and appears normal for 15-20 minutes before the next episode. Vomiting, pallor, and pulling up the legs are common. Passage of reddish bloody mucus — called redcurrant jelly stool — appears hours later.

faqs.question: How is intussusception treated?
faqs.answer: In children without complications, a doctor pushes pressurised air or fluid into the rectum to push the telescoped bowel back to its normal position; this works in 70-90% of cases and does not require an operation. If this fails, or if the bowel has been damaged, surgery is needed to manually undo the telescoping or remove the affected segment.

faqs.question: Is intussusception an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intussusception cuts off the blood supply to the trapped bowel; without treatment within hours, the bowel can become permanently damaged and perforated. Any infant with episodic severe crying, pallor, and vomiting should be taken to an emergency department immediately, as early treatment gives the best chance of avoiding surgery.

faqs.question: Can intussusception come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 10-15% of children have a recurrence after successful enema reduction, almost always within 48-72 hours of going home. Parents are warned to return immediately if the episodic crying and pallor come back. A second enema is usually successful; three or more recurrences may prompt surgery to look for an underlying structural cause.

faqs.question: What causes intussusception in children?
faqs.answer: In most children under five no specific cause is found; it is thought that the gut's lymph tissue swells after a viral cold or stomach bug and catches on the bowel wall, triggering telescoping. In older children and adults, a physical reason such as a polyp, a Meckel's diverticulum, a tumour, or thickened bowel from Crohn's disease is usually responsible.

faqs.question: What is the air enema for intussusception?
faqs.answer: An air enema is a procedure where a doctor places a small tube in the rectum and pumps in air under controlled pressure while watching on an X-ray or ultrasound screen. The air gradually pushes the telescoped piece of bowel back out to its normal position. It is quick, safe in most cases, and avoids the need for surgery in the majority of children.

faqs.question: What is the difference between intussusception and volvulus?
faqs.answer: In intussusception, one piece of intestine slides inside another like a telescope — most common in infants. In volvulus, a loop of intestine twists around itself — more common in older adults (sigmoid volvulus) or newborns (midgut volvulus). Both block the bowel and cut off blood flow and are surgical emergencies, but they are treated differently.

faqs.question: Can intussusception happen in adults?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it accounts for only about 5% of all cases. In adults, an underlying cause is found in over 90% of cases — most commonly a bowel polyp, colorectal cancer, or a tumour in the small bowel. Adult intussusception almost always requires surgery to remove the obstructed segment and any pathological cause.

faqs.question: What is the redcurrant jelly stool of intussusception?
faqs.answer: As the blood supply to the trapped bowel is compressed, the bowel lining bleeds and sheds mucus; these mix in the rectum and are passed as dark red, jellylike material. This is a late sign appearing hours after other symptoms and indicates established vascular compromise. Its presence is a strong indicator for emergency treatment.

faqs.question: How is intussusception diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Abdominal ultrasound is the first test in children and is over 95% accurate; it shows a characteristic 'target' or 'doughnut' ring pattern where the two bowel layers are visible side by side. In adults, CT scanning is used and shows the same multilayered pattern along with any underlying lead-point lesion. Plain X-rays can suggest the diagnosis but are not specific enough on their own.

faqs.question: Does intussusception require surgery in children?
faqs.answer: Not always. About 70-90% of children with uncomplicated intussusception are successfully treated without surgery using an air or fluid enema. Surgery is needed when the enema fails, when the bowel is perforated, when there are signs of bowel death, or when an underlying structural cause is found that must be removed.

faqs.question: Can the rotavirus vaccine cause intussusception?
faqs.answer: The first rotavirus vaccine, Rotashield, was withdrawn in 1999 because it raised intussusception risk significantly. Current vaccines (Rotarix, RotaTeq) carry a very small risk — about 1-2 extra cases per 100,000 doses. This risk is far smaller than the deaths and severe illness that rotavirus itself causes, so international health bodies continue to recommend the vaccine for all infants.

faqs.question: What happens if intussusception is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, the compressed blood vessels cause the trapped bowel to die within hours to days, leading to perforation and peritonitis — a life-threatening abdominal infection. The risk of bowel death rises sharply after about six hours of complete obstruction. Untreated intussusception is fatal without surgical intervention.

faqs.question: Is intussusception painful for a baby?
faqs.answer: Yes, significantly. The colicky waves of pain cause intense distress — the infant screams and pulls the knees up sharply. Between spasms, when the intussuscepted bowel temporarily relaxes, the child may appear completely calm or even drowsy, which can mislead parents into thinking the problem has resolved. Each pain wave lasts one to two minutes and recurs every 15-20 minutes.

faqs.question: What is a lead point in intussusception?
faqs.answer: A lead point is a structural abnormality on the bowel wall — such as a polyp, Meckel's diverticulum, or tumour — that gets caught by the squeezing motion of peristalsis and dragged downstream, pulling the surrounding bowel inside itself. Lead points are found in fewer than 10% of children under five but in over 90% of adult cases.

faqs.question: How long does a child stay in hospital for intussusception?
faqs.answer: After successful enema reduction without complications, most children are observed for four to six hours, fed, and discharged home the same day or after an overnight stay. After laparoscopic surgery, discharge within two to four days is typical. After open surgery with bowel resection, the stay is five to seven days, with full recovery over four to six weeks.

faqs.question: Can intussusception be prevented?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic paediatric intussusception cannot reliably be prevented. Rotavirus vaccination reduces the viral gastroenteritis that precedes many cases. In adults, regular colonoscopy and polyp removal reduces the number of potential lead points. For children with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome or familial polyposis, regular endoscopic polypectomy reduces intussusception risk substantially.

faqs.question: What is Meckel's diverticulum and its link to intussusception?
faqs.answer: A Meckel's diverticulum is a small finger-like pouch on the small intestine present from birth in about 2% of people. It can become inflamed or inverted, acting as a lead point that triggers telescoping of the bowel. It is the most common structural cause of intussusception in children aged 2-12 and is typically removed at surgery when found.

faqs.question: When should I take my child to the emergency room for suspected intussusception?
faqs.answer: Take your child to the emergency room immediately if they have episodes of extreme crying every 15-20 minutes with the legs drawn up and pallor, especially combined with vomiting or dark bloody mucus in the nappy. Do not wait to see if it settles — the window for non-surgical treatment is short and delays increase the risk of bowel damage requiring surgery.

metaDescription: Intussusception is when the bowel telescopes into itself, causing obstruction and ischaemia. Learn symptoms, enema reduction and surgery. Doctor-reviewed.
